extra.message: User-controlled post_id is concatenated directly into an SQL query string via 'SELECT * FROM posts WHERE id = '+post_id. Although a regex check exists on line 67, it uses re.M (multiline) flag making it bypassable with newline characters (e.g., '1\n OR 1=1--'). This allows SQL injection.

The regex r'^\d+$' with re.M (multiline) flag allows bypass. The ^ and $ anchors match per-line instead of per-string, so input like '1\n<SQL injection>' passes the check because the first line '1' satisfies the pattern. This weakens the input validation intended to prevent SQL injection.

Flask SECRET_KEY is hardcoded as 'qwerty12345'. This key is used for cryptographically signing session cookies. A known secret key allows an attacker to forge session cookies and bypass authentication.
Flask SECRET_KEY is hardcoded as '55555'. This key is used for cryptographically signing session cookies. A known secret key allows an attacker to forge session cookies and bypass authentication.

User-controlled self.path is directly appended to the filesystem path (path += self.path). The only protection is a weak regex on line 10 (r'/\.\.\/\.\.\/') that only blocks exactly '/../../' at the start (re.match). This is trivially bypassed: single '../' traversal, path prefixes like '/foo/../../', or URL-encoded variants. An attacker can read arbitrary files on the system.

cwe: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')

extra.message: The username value, derived from decrypting a user-supplied cookie, is inserted directly into HTML via 'Hello %s' % username without any escaping or encoding. Since the cookie value is attacker-controlled (and the encryption lacks integrity protection), an attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript via CBC bit-flipping or by crafting a malicious cookie, leading to reflected XSS.

The decrypt function processes user-supplied encrypted cookie data using AES-CBC without any integrity check (MAC/HMAC). This enables padding oracle attacks to decrypt cookies and CBC bit-flipping attacks to modify the plaintext, both without detection. Combined with the XSS on line 37, this allows an attacker to inject arbitrary content into the page.

cwe: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

extra.message: User-controlled URL from request.args.get('url') is passed to urllib.request.urlopen() with only a weak startswith('https://insecurewebapp.com') check. This is bypassable via: (1) subdomain takeover: 'https://insecurewebapp.com.evil.com', (2) URL authority: 'https://insecurewebapp.com@evil.com', or (3) any subdomain under insecurewebapp.com. An attacker can make the server fetch arbitrary internal or external resources.

Payment signature is computed as SHA-256(secret + payment_information) instead of using HMAC. This construction is vulnerable to hash length extension attacks: an attacker who knows the hash output and the length of the secret can append additional data to the payment_information and compute a valid signature without knowing the secret. This allows tampering with payment details (e.g., changing the amount).

Payment signature is computed as SHA-256(secret + payment_information) instead of HMAC. This hash(secret||message) construction is vulnerable to hash length extension attacks, allowing an attacker to append data to payment_information and compute a valid signature without knowing the secret.

SSL hostname verification is explicitly disabled with context.check_hostname = False. This allows man-in-the-middle attacks where an attacker presents a valid certificate for a different domain, and the connection will still be accepted. The code also disables other verification by not setting context.verify_mode.

The commented-out code on line 15 shows pickle.loads(base64.b64decode(cookies.get('username').value)) being called on user-supplied cookie data. The code comments explicitly note: 'Calls pickle.loads on user supplied info - No signature present or anything preventing the sending of a malicious pickle object - This would enable code execution.' The active Username.pickle() call on line 14 and the class structure indicate this deserialization pattern is intended for use. Insecure deserialization of untrusted data enables remote code execution.

The username variable is inserted into HTML via '

Hello %s

' % username without escaping. While currently the else branch sets username to the hardcoded 'stranger', the intended code pattern (commented out on line 15) would derive username from user-supplied cookie data. When the deserialization code is active, this becomes a stored XSS vulnerability.

HTTP server is bound to 0.0.0.0 (all network interfaces), making the vulnerable path traversal service accessible from any network. Combined with the path traversal vulnerability, this exposes arbitrary file reads to any network attacker.

cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

extra.message: HTTP server is bound to 0.0.0.0 (all network interfaces), making the insecure deserialization and XSS service accessible from any network. The code comment on line 36 acknowledges this: 'Issue:B104:hardcoded_bind_all_interfaces Possible binding to all interfaces.'

cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
